title: Gmail Plus-Addressing Enumeration for Multi-Account Abuse
narrative: Fraudster creates 8-15 accounts using a single Gmail address with plus-addressing variants (baseuser+var1@gmail.com, baseuser+var2@gmail.com, etc.) to exploit new-user promotions or distribute suspicious activity across apparent identities. Each variant appears as a unique P_emaildomain entry but resolves to the same inbox. Attacks typically unfold within 48-72 hours: accounts created sequentially, then used for coordinated purchases targeting gift cards or high-resale electronics. TransactionAmt clusters tightly around $299-$349 per variant account. All accounts share identical device fingerprints (DeviceInfo) and originate from the same addr1/dist1 combination. Card funding spreads across 3-5 different card1 BINs to avoid single-card velocity flags, but card1_txn_count_24h remains artificially low per card. Purchases concentrate in late evening hours (txn_hour 22-02) with is_night=1, suggesting batch scripting. R_emaildomain mismatches P_emaildomain in 60%+ of transactions.
indicators: 8-15 P_emaildomain values sharing identical base address before '+' symbol, all gmail.com
All associated accounts created within 48-72 hour window
Identical DeviceInfo hash across all plus-addressed accounts
TransactionAmt clusters $299-$349 with <$15 standard deviation across accounts
Card1_txn_count_24h stays under 3 per card, but 3-5 different card1 BINs funding the email cluster
R_emaildomain differs from P_emaildomain in 60%+ of transactions for this email cluster
summary: A single Gmail address spawns 8-15 accounts using plus-addressing variants (user+1@gmail.com, user+2@gmail.com), all sharing the same device fingerprint and shipping address while making near-identical purchases ($299-$349) within a 48-72 hour window. Transactions cluster in late-night hours and spread across multiple payment cards to stay under velocity thresholds.
reasoning: The fraudster exploits Gmail's plus-addressing feature to create the illusion of multiple customers and repeatedly claim new-user promotions or circumvent account limits, while the shared device and address prove it's one actor orchestrating a coordinated attack on high-resale goods.
indicator_explanations: Same Gmail base address reused with plus-sign tricks to create fake accounts
All suspect accounts were registered in a concentrated time burst
Every account logs in from the exact same device fingerprint
Transaction amounts are suspiciously uniform across all the linked accounts
Multiple payment cards fund the scheme while keeping each card's activity low
Fraudster operates almost exclusively during late-night hours to avoid detection
Recipient email differs from purchaser email in most transactions across cluster
